sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.stem: Which interpretation is best supported by the pattern shown in the figure from the neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake study?

choices.A: The figure proves that neighborhood deprivation index alone directly caused the outcome in every participant, with no remaining confounding or mediation.
choices.B: The figure supports a graded association consistent with social determinants of health, while leaving room for partial mediation by the proposed mechanism.
choices.C: The figure shows that the intervention or highest-risk condition had no meaningful relation to completed preventive follow-up visit, so the study hypothesis is unsupported.
choices.D: The figure is best interpreted as a pure biological maturation effect that does not require any social, cognitive, or behavioral explanation.

explanations.A: This option is plausible because it uses language that appears in the same general content area, but it does not match the passage's actual comparison. It either reverses the direction of the figure, treats a correlational design as a definitive causal experiment, or substitutes a related concept for the mechanism under study. For this question, that matters because the passage focuses on social determinants of health, not merely on any psychological or sociological term that could be associated with neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake.
explanations.B: Correct. This choice best integrates the passage design with the figure rather than treating the passage as a vocabulary-matching exercise. The relevant comparison concerns neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake, and the data pattern is interpreted through social determinants of health. The correct answer also respects the limits of the study: the passage supports a measured association or a plausible mechanism, not an unlimited causal conclusion. Because the question asks about figure, the strongest response must explain why the observed pattern follows from the experimental or observational setup and why the named construct, social stratification, is being used in a specific way.
explanations.C: This option is plausible because it uses language that appears in the same general content area, but it does not match the passage's actual comparison. It either reverses the direction of the figure, treats a correlational design as a definitive causal experiment, or substitutes a related concept for the mechanism under study. For this question, that matters because the passage focuses on social determinants of health, not merely on any psychological or sociological term that could be associated with neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake.
explanations.D: This option is plausible because it uses language that appears in the same general content area, but it does not match the passage's actual comparison. It either reverses the direction of the figure, treats a correlational design as a definitive causal experiment, or substitutes a related concept for the mechanism under study. For this question, that matters because the passage focuses on social determinants of health, not merely on any psychological or sociological term that could be associated with neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake.

questions.topic: Neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake
questions.passage: Researchers investigated neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake in community primary-care clinics serving adults with hypertension. The investigators framed the project around social determinants of health, but they were careful not to treat the measured association as a simple individual-choice problem. Participants completed baseline surveys, and administrative records were used when possible so that self-report error would not be the only source of information. The primary independent variable was neighborhood deprivation index; the primary outcome was completed preventive follow-up visit. Because the investigators expected background differences among participants, they also measured age, baseline health status, prior service use, and a short index of neighborhood resources. Participants were assigned or grouped into four comparison conditions: Low deprivation, Moderate deprivation, High deprivation, High + navigator. The figure summarizes the main outcome values: Low deprivation: 74; Moderate deprivation: 61; High deprivation: 42; High + navigator: 58. The pattern was not interpreted as proof that the first condition directly caused all later differences. Instead, the researchers argued that the results were most consistent with the idea that completion fell as deprivation increased, but a navigator program partially recovered follow-up in the high-deprivation group. They also emphasized that the absolute values should be read in light of the study setting, because the sample came from institutions that had already agreed to participate in a behavioral or social-science project. A secondary analysis tested whether transportation reliability and perceived clinic discrimination helped explain the observed pattern. The researchers used regression models that added potential mediators after the main association was estimated. When these mediators were included, the coefficient for neighborhood deprivation index was reduced but not eliminated. The authors interpreted this as partial mediation rather than complete mediation. They also tested an interaction term to see whether the association differed across demographic subgroups, but confidence intervals for the interaction were wide, so they described that analysis as exploratory rather than confirmatory. Several design features limited interpretation. The study measured many variables after enrollment, so reverse causation and selection into the study could not be fully excluded. Some measures were objective, but others depended on participants' willingness to report sensitive experiences accurately. The investigators attempted to reduce demand characteristics by telling participants that the study concerned general decision-making rather than a preferred hypothesis. Even with this precaution, they noted that social desirability could influence responses, especially when questions involved stigma, compliance, or institutional trust. The authors concluded that the findings were useful for generating interventions but not sufficient for making broad causal claims. They proposed a follow-up study that would manipulate a practical barrier or communication frame while holding background variables constant. They also recommended measuring both immediate responses and delayed behavior, because a change in attitude or intention may not translate into behavior if structural barriers remain.
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Follow-up completion (%) by condition in the neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake study.
questions.passageHelp: This distinction was important: a psychological construct such as social stratification could be relevant, but interpreting the figure required connecting that construct to the specific research design rather than relying on vocabulary alone.

questions.stem: Which limitation most directly prevents the investigators from making the strongest causal claim about neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake?

choices.A: The study is invalid because behavioral outcomes can never be measured quantitatively, even when the outcome is recorded prospectively., although this interpretation would require evidence beyond the study's measured comparison
choices.B: The study cannot be interpreted because any use of regression automatically removes all information about mechanisms and subgroup patterns.
choices.C: Because participants were not fully randomized to all naturally occurring exposure conditions, unmeasured differences could still influence both the exposure and the outcome.
choices.D: The main limitation is that descriptive statistics are always less informative than case reports when researchers study health behavior or social context.

explanations.B: This option is plausible because it uses language that appears in the same general content area, but it does not match the passage's actual comparison. explanations.C: Correct. This choice best integrates the passage design with the figure rather than treating the passage as a vocabulary-matching exercise. The relevant comparison concerns neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake, and the data pattern is interpreted through social determinants of health. The correct answer also respects the limits of the study: the passage supports a measured association or a plausible mechanism, not an unlimited causal conclusion. questions.topic: Neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake

questions.stem: The passage's discussion of transportation reliability and perceived clinic discrimination most directly reflects which concept?

choices.A: individual-level health belief, because any study that includes human participants must be interpreted primarily through that unrelated construct., although this interpretation would require evidence beyond the study's measured comparison
choices.B: A ceiling effect, because the passage states that all groups reached the maximum possible value on the outcome measure.
choices.C: Random assignment, because the passage's observational comparisons guarantee that preexisting differences were eliminated entirely.
choices.D: social stratification, because the passage connects a measured pattern to a named social or psychological process operating within the study context.

questions.topic: Neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake

questions.stem: If the proposed mechanism in the neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake passage is correct, which follow-up result would be most expected?

choices.A: Changing the mechanism-related barrier should reverse all group differences immediately, because social and psychological processes never show delayed effects., although this interpretation would require evidence beyond the study's measured comparison
choices.B: Reducing the mechanism-related barrier should weaken the association between neighborhood deprivation index and completed preventive follow-up visit without necessarily eliminating every group difference.
choices.C: The association should become stronger after the barrier is reduced, since removing a mediator generally increases the unexplained portion of the original effect.
choices.D: The outcome should be identical across all subgroups before any intervention occurs, because measured disparities cannot coexist with psychological mechanisms.

questions.topic: Neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake

questions.stem: Which additional finding would most strengthen the authors' interpretation of neighborhood deprivation and preventive care uptake?

choices.A: A result showing that the same outcome pattern appears when the proposed mechanism is not measured and all contextual variables are ignored.
choices.B: A cross-sectional subgroup difference that is statistically significant but unrelated to neighborhood deprivation index, completed preventive follow-up visit, or the proposed mechanism.
choices.C: A manipulation check showing only that participants remembered the survey instructions, with no change in the mediator or outcome.
choices.D: A mediation analysis showing that changes in transportation reliability and perceived clinic discrimination account for part of the observed group difference in the outcome.

choices.D: The figure is best interpreted as a pure biological maturation effect that does not require any social, cognitive, or behavioral explanation.

questions.topic: Insurance churn and delayed diabetes care
questions.passage: Researchers investigated insurance churn and delayed diabetes care in patients with type 2 diabetes enrolled in a safety-net system. The investigators framed the project around health disparities, but they were careful not to treat the measured association as a simple individual-choice problem. Participants completed baseline surveys, and administrative records were used when possible so that self-report error would not be the only source of information. The primary independent variable was insurance stability over twelve months; the primary outcome was mean delay before medication adjustment. Because the investigators expected background differences among participants, they also measured age, baseline health status, prior service use, and a short index of neighborhood resources. Participants were assigned or grouped into four comparison conditions: Stable private, Stable Medicaid, Churned coverage, Uninsured gap. The figure summarizes the main outcome values: Stable private: 11; Stable Medicaid: 18; Churned coverage: 31; Uninsured gap: 39. The pattern was not interpreted as proof that the first condition directly caused all later differences. Instead, the researchers argued that the results were most consistent with the idea that patients with coverage interruptions had the longest delays despite similar baseline A1c values. They also emphasized that the absolute values should be read in light of the study setting, because the sample came from institutions that had already agreed to participate in a behavioral or social-science project. A secondary analysis tested whether institutional barriers and discontinuity of access helped explain the observed pattern. The researchers used regression models that added potential mediators after the main association was estimated. When these mediators were included, the coefficient for insurance stability over twelve months was reduced but not eliminated. The authors interpreted this as partial mediation rather than complete mediation. They also tested an interaction term to see whether the association differed across demographic subgroups, but confidence intervals for the interaction were wide, so they described that analysis as exploratory rather than confirmatory. Several design features limited interpretation. The study measured many variables after enrollment, so reverse causation and selection into the study could not be fully excluded. Some measures were objective, but others depended on participants' willingness to report sensitive experiences accurately. The investigators attempted to reduce demand characteristics by telling participants that the study concerned general decision-making rather than a preferred hypothesis. Even with this precaution, they noted that social desirability could influence responses, especially when questions involved stigma, compliance, or institutional trust. The authors concluded that the findings were useful for generating interventions but not sufficient for making broad causal claims. They proposed a follow-up study that would manipulate a practical barrier or communication frame while holding background variables constant. They also recommended measuring both immediate responses and delayed behavior, because a change in attitude or intention may not translate into behavior if structural barriers remain.

questions.passageHelp: This distinction was important: a psychological construct such as structural barrier could be relevant, but interpreting the figure required connecting that construct to the specific research design rather than relying on vocabulary alone.
